steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Boundaries question testing punctuation between a verb and its direct object. The sentence reads: 'Her innovative poems ______ the use of Jamaican Creole (a spoken language) in literature.' The blank is filled by 'popularized' plus whatever punctuation follows it. A verb must not be separated from its direct object by any punctuation.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — popularized;: A semicolon is used between two independent clauses. 'The use of Jamaican Creole in literature' is not an independent clause; it is the direct object of 'popularized.' A semicolon here breaks the sentence illegally. ✗
  • Choice B — popularized,: A comma between a verb and its direct object is not standard in English. No comma is warranted here. ✗
  • Choice C — popularized: No punctuation between the verb and its direct object. This conforms to Standard English conventions. ✓
  • Choice D — popularized:: A colon can introduce a list or explanation, but 'the use of Jamaican Creole in literature' is simply the direct object of 'popularized,' not a list or a restatement requiring a colon. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
